research_scope: Historical grappling actions integrated into armed combat: weapon grabs (shaft/blade), arm/wrist grabs, and pins. Facts only -- no proposed formulas, gains, or 0-1 scalars.

engine_context_reviewed.wrapper_py: C:/Github/ttrpg-morph-rearch/designs/scene/combat_engine_v1/wrapper.py -- engagement() state machine. Approach phase (~line 86-109): measure_gap closes via close_rate; a longer weapon threatens with stop-hits. Closed phase (~line 110-303): tempo-gated exchanges, bind (3-iteration Fuhlen/leverage contest), riposte, reopen-moment logic for the longer weapon to regain distance (including a 'freed-hand shove' push_avail path for 2H weapons at PUSH_AVAIL_P, wrapper.py:249-252). NO grapple/clinch/pin state exists anywhere in this state machine today.
engine_context_reviewed.systems_py: C:/Github/ttrpg-morph-rearch/designs/scene/combat_engine_v1/systems.py -- confirmed existing primitives: reach_base() (geometric standing reach from head_len+grip_len), close_rate()/approach_displace() (closing-the-gap mechanics via leverage differential), leverage() (explicit hand-to-contact lever-arm, grip_len minus a fraction of head_len, 2H bonus), commit_depth() (Beta-distributed [2,5] commitment/recovery spectrum), recoverability_factor() (grip-aware MoI-based balance-recovery), at_grip()/grip_position (continuous choke-up-the-haft state, grip_target() derives how far a fighter gathers in once closed, bounded by WP.grip_choke_max), bind_sigma() (leverage+blade_guard 'catch'+tactile Fuhlen contest in the bind). NONE of these model grabbing an opponent's weapon/limb or a pin -- leverage/bind_sigma model redirecting/controlling THROUGH weapon-to-weapon contact, not seizing bare-handed.
engine_context_reviewed.clinch_field_status: weapons.py defines clinch=<1-10 int> on every weapon record (comment: 'grappling affinity') but grep across the entire designs/scene/combat_engine_v1/ tree confirms ZERO functions reference it -- no clinch_sigma, no disengage, no choke function exists (phase4_5_plan_v1.md:216-218, explicitly flagged NOT STARTED). Sampled clinch values: dagger=10, rondel=9, estoc_halfsword=9, misericorde=7, stiletto=7, bec_de_corbin=8, lucerne_hammer=7, longsword_halfsword=7, longsword=6, kama_yari=6, ranseur=6, flamberge=6, spear=2, rapier=2, guisarme=2, ji=3 (despite ji's documented hooking crescent, see polearm section below), poleaxe=5, mace=4, staff=3. The field is currently DEAD DATA -- no consumer.
engine_context_reviewed.prior_repo_proposal_not_source: C:/Github/ttrpg/designs/audit/2026-05-29-combat-armature/grappling_system_design.md is a prior Class-C DESIGN PROPOSAL (not ratified, not a factual source) sketching a Strength-dominant grapple contest + a 'grounded-foe dagger to the gaps' finish citing Fiore abrazare/Ringen/the rondel-dagger record in general terms. It is cited here only to note it exists and should not be duplicated as fact-grounding; none of its specific mechanics (contest formula, margin-scaled outcomes) are treated as historically attested below.

findings.topic: Fiore dei Liberi -- Abrazare (unarmed grappling), overview
facts: Fiore dei Liberi's Fior di Battaglia / Flos Duellatorum (earliest complete redaction dated 1409; principal manuscripts: Getty MS Ludwig XV 13, Pisani-Dossi, Morgan M.383, Paris BnF) opens its martial system with Abrazare (grappling/wrestling), which Fiore treats as the foundational art underlying dagger, sword, and armoured combat.

Abrazare technique content: throws, holds, joint locks (arm bars/breaks), binds, and disarms, executed from the fighters already having 'come to grips' (i.e. the plays begin in contact, not at a distance).

As a system evolved for armoured wrestling, strikes are minimal in Abrazare; chokes and wrist-locks (in the sense of small-joint wrist manipulation as a primary finishing lock) are notably ABSENT, attributed to the practical realities of fighting an armoured opponent where such locks are less effective through/around armour.

Abrazare technique is explicitly cross-applied: Fiore reuses Abrazare locks/throws inside his dagger plays and his close-play (Zogho Stretto) sword plays -- the grappling vocabulary is the same whether the hands are empty or a weapon is present.

findings.topic: Fiore dei Liberi -- measure/range conditions: Zogho Largo vs Zogho Stretto
facts: Fiore defines two measures (zoghi) for two-handed sword. Zogho Largo (wide play): swords meet at or near their tips; fencers stand at the outer edge of weapon reach with arms extended; neither can directly threaten the other with the point without a step first. Footwork signature: left foot forward. NO grappling is available at this measure -- it operates within sword-vs-sword technique only.

Zogho Stretto (narrow/close play): the swords cross near the base/hilt (a 'crossing' / bind), fencers are within arm's reach of each other -- close enough that a pommel strike can reach the opponent's face. Fiore's own description (as rendered by multiple translators): the close play of the sword in two hands teaches 'every kind of parry, strike, bind, dislocation, grapple, disarm and throw' with remedies/counters to each. Footwork signature: right foot forward, closing the dominant hand toward the opponent.

CRITICAL RANGE FINDING: in the Getty-manuscript-derived close-play (Zogho Stretto) sequence, all 25 plays are explicitly stated to proceed FROM AN EXISTING CROSSING/BIND with the right foot forward ('The first play I execute derives from my Master's crossing with his right foot forward') -- i.e. Fiore's sword-grappling plays are NOT reached for from open measure; they begin already in blade-to-blade contact that has compressed to close range. The techniques (grabbing the sword arm, body, or head; hilt/shaft strikes; body-to-body throws) execute FROM that established crossing, largely without an additional step.

For the dagger, by contrast, Abrazare-style grabs (of the weapon-bearing wrist/arm) are the OPENING action against an incoming dagger thrust -- i.e. at dagger range the grab IS the first contact, not something requiring a prior bind (see 1st Master finding below). Range condition therefore differs by weapon class: sword-vs-sword grappling is bind-gated (Zogho Stretto); dagger-vs-dagger/unarmed grappling begins at the first exchange of contact.

UNCERTAINTY FLAG: sources describing the Zogho Stretto transition are secondary/tertiary (HEMA-club summaries, one dedicated blog post) rather than a direct quote of Fiore's prologue text on the two measures; treat the 'requires established bind, no additional step' framing as well-corroborated across multiple independent secondary sources but not independently verified against a primary-manuscript folio citation in this pass.

findings.topic: Fiore dei Liberi -- sword plays transitioning into grappling: disarms, arm-locks (Zogho Stretto)
facts: At the half-sword / close crossing, Fiore explicitly states that on passing forward he will perform 'binds, disarms and grapples' -- i.e. the same technical family (binding leverage, disarming, grappling) is presented as one continuum, not three separate systems.

Documented technique pattern (secondary-source paraphrase of Getty-derived plays): the defending scholar 'hooks his hand over the opponent's wrist and grabs his blade in his left hand, pressing it forward into the enemy's neck while wrenching back with the hilt' -- i.e. a simultaneous wrist-grab (controlling the attacking arm) plus an off-hand blade-grab (controlling the weapon itself), combined into one action, driving the opponent's own weapon/hilt into their neck.

Named arm-lock/pin mechanics: 'posta lunga over the grabbing arm, putting the same arm into a vertical middle key against the fighter's torso, supported by the other arm' -- describes pinning the opponent's captured arm against the practitioner's own torso using body-weight/structure, not a joint-only lock. Another variant: 'forces the opponent's chin back with posta lunga or seizes the opponent's wrist while applying posta lunga to the chin' -- the sword's own extended-arm position (posta lunga) is used as the pinning/control lever against the head/neck while the other hand secures the wrist.

Throw-from-the-bind: at the fully compressed Zogho Stretto distance, 'a throw from the bind puts the opponent on the ground and the fight ends there' -- corroborates that once the grapple/throw lands, the play is presented as terminal (no further exchange modeled), consistent with the general HEMA pattern of grappling being decisive rather than iterative.

The rompere di punta ('breaking the thrust') play, documented for the spear but explicitly derived by Fiore from his sword defenses against the thrust: after beating the opponent's weapon offline (to the ground), the defender 'forcefully places [a] foot over his [opponent's] sword. Either I break it or I hold it in [such] a way that he cannot control it any more' -- i.e. a FOOT-PIN of the opponent's weapon against the ground, immediately followed (if the pin alone does not suffice) by a strike to the neck with the flat/false edge, then a follow-up cut to the arms/hands. Getty Ms., Carta 28V (page 26v).
sources: http://learnfiore.org/wp-content/uploads/StrettoPlays.pdf (Gregory D. Mele, Chicago Swordplay Guild -- direct citation to Getty Ms. Ludwig XV.13, Carta 28V)

findings.topic: Fiore dei Liberi -- Dagger 1st and 2nd Remedy Masters: wrist-grab-into-disarm and 'the grip so strong'
facts: Dagger 1st Master (Getty Ms., described as folio ~10v; 'the remedy'): against an incoming (mandritto/fendente-style overhead) dagger thrust, the defender contacts the attacker's forearm/wrist AT THE APEX of the strike, then 'roll[s] your hand over and grab on' to secure the opponent's wrist. This grab is executed from OPEN CONTACT (the moment of the incoming strike), not from a pre-established bind -- the first physical contact of the exchange IS the grab.

Mechanism of the resulting disarm: keeping the grabbing palm facing down and drawing the elbow in close to the hip, the defender applies rotational torque (generated from the blocking motion) to 'lever the dagger out of the [attacker's] hand.' This is a lever-arm/torque action on the captured wrist, not raw grip-strength alone.

The First Master's own stated principle: 'I am the First Master and called the remedy. Because it is a remedy to so much, I say that in understanding this remedy, you cannot hurt me and that I can strike and hurt you' -- i.e. the entire dagger-grapple system is framed around denying the attacker (control of the weapon arm) while opening them to a counter.

Second Remedy Master (secondary-source translation, consistent across multiple citing sites): 'This grip is so strong that I believe I can kill anyone with it, because I can break your arm and I can throw you to the ground, and I can take your dagger. I can also tie you in the high bind. And from these four things, you will not be free.' -- names FOUR distinct outcomes available from a single dominant grip/control: (1) arm-break, (2) throw/takedown, (3) disarm, (4) a tying/binding restraint ('high bind'), explicitly framed as a decision point once control is achieved, not a single fixed outcome.

This Second Master's grip is described as working especially well against an overhead hammerfist-style dagger attack (mandritto/fendente), i.e. success is tied to a specific attack geometry (a committed overhead strike gives the defender's grab a clean line to the incoming wrist).

findings.topic: German tradition -- Ringen am Schwert (wrestling at the sword)
facts: Ringen am Schwert ('grappling at the sword') is documented as a specialized branch of Kampfringen (combat wrestling) built specifically to operate during armed sword combat, combining closing techniques, disarms, weapon-seizures, pommel-strikes, and weapon-aided joint-locks.

RANGE CONDITION (explicit in Ringeck's text as translated by ARMA/thearma.org): entry into Ringen am Schwert techniques is triggered 'When you bind against his sword' -- i.e. the German tradition's sword-grappling techniques, like Fiore's Zogho Stretto, flow FROM an established bind/blade engagement, not from open measure. When the distance closes beyond cutting or thrusting range (i.e. the bind has compressed further), fighters transition to using their crossguards, pommels, and half-swording grips to throw, disarm, or lock the opponent.

Named technique -- Verkehrer (documented in Ringeck's commentary on Liechtenauer): 'If you bind at your adversary's sword with an Oberhau or an Unterhau, turn your sword so that your thumb is down and thrust to his face from above' -- a bind-entry winding action, precursor to the grapple sequence rather than a grab itself.

Named technique -- the 'Fifth Sword Wrestle' (Ringeck): the defender drives the left hand OVER the opponent's right arm and seizes the opponent's own sword handle (hilt) between both hands -- an explicit two-handed HILT GRAB executed from the bind, seizing the opponent's weapon at the grip rather than the blade.

Named technique -- 'Taking a Sword' (a Schwertnehmen / sword-taking action, Ringeck): 'seize both swords in the centre of the blades with the left [gloved/protected] hand. Hold them together firmly, and drive through with your right hand' -- an explicit MID-BLADE grab (grabbing both crossed blades together at their center) used to immobilize the bind and free the right hand for a follow-up (a mordhau/pommel strike or throw). This confirms blade-grabbing (not just hilt-grabbing) is a documented action, historically reliant on gloved/armoured hands or grabbing at a point of the blade where leverage/danger is minimized.

Named technique -- Leg-breaks and Under-breaks (Ringeck): multiple techniques seize the opponent's hand/arm 'behind or at the biceps' as the entry point for a throw -- i.e. the upper arm (not just the wrist) is a documented grab target for leverage into a throw.

Named technique -- a 'finger-lever/hold': 'Seize one of his hands with both your hands, in any case two fingers right next to each other, and tear them apart' -- a small-joint (finger) lock/break used as a release-forcing technique, distinct from the larger-joint arm-locks.

Named technique -- 'Second Leg-break': breaking the opponent's captured arm 'by lifting it onto the shoulder while turning the back' -- a shoulder-fulcrum arm-break, i.e. the practitioner's own body (shoulder) is used as the lever fulcrum against the captured limb (directly analogous to Fiore's torso-braced arm-pin above).

Escape/counter options for the grabbed fighter (general pattern, not exhaustively itemized per play in the sources reviewed): the source material states grabbed fighters may 'counter-grip, escape through footwork manipulation, or transition to alternative wrestling positions' and that the Ringeck text 'frequently includes defensive counters' to its own wrestling plays -- confirming counters/escapes are a documented, not merely implied, part of the system, though this pass did not extract a specific named escape technique with citation.

Ott Jud (also 'Ott the Jew') is a wrestling master of the early 15th century credited across multiple German fechtbücher (no surviving treatise in his own hand; his system is preserved via later masters including Hans Talhoffer, Peter von Danzig, and Jud Lew, and appears in manuscripts such as the Codex Lew) with a body of technique described as joint breaks, arm locks, and throws.

Ott Jud's system (per one secondary characterization) 'features forceful arm grabs and hip throws to aggressively disrupt balance early,' described as reflecting an offensively-oriented Austrian/German emphasis on quick dominance in the opening of an unarmed exchange, in some framings contrasted with Ringeck's own wrestling material (a different regional emphasis).

UNCERTAINTY FLAG: this pass could not retrieve specific named/numbered Ott Jud plays with direct manuscript-page citation (Wiktenauer, the primary aggregator for this content, returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch both for the general Fiore page and the Ott Jud page specifically). The characterization above rests on secondary summary only and should be treated as directional, not a verified specific-technique citation.
sources: web-search-derived summary; Wiktenauer direct access blocked (403 Forbidden) for https://wiktenauer.com/wiki/Ott_Jud

findings.topic: Polearm-specific grappling: hooking morphology and hook-into-grapple/pull-down actions
facts: PROJECT DATA CONFIRMED (weapons.py, read directly): eight weapons in the roster carry morphology-rearch Phase B2 'mode_elements' -- physically located, independently-geometried striking elements grounded against specimen research: poleaxe, bec_de_corbin, lucerne_hammer, ji, goedendag, guisarme, kama_yari, voulge. Of these, several carry an explicit 'dual_role_element' guard/catching-hardware flag documenting a hooking or catching surface as a DISTINCT physical feature from the striking elements: guisarme's 'hooked cutting blade' (x_m=0.6958, extent_m=0.32, comment: 'the hook itself is the weapon's namesake catching feature'); ji's 'perpendicular crescent' (x_m=0.7586, comment: 'documented hooking surface used to unhorse cavalry/catch shields'); bec_de_corbin's rear-facing 'beak/fluke' (curved spike opposite the hammer face); lucerne_hammer's '3-4 tine fluke (rear beak)'; ranseur's 'left side prong/wing' (x_m=1.02, comment: 'documented bind-catching surface, [cites] Monte'); partisan's 'left wing-lug' (x_m=0.9, comment: 'documented active trap-and-bind catching surface, [cites] Manciolino'); voulge's 'rear spike/hook notch' (comment: 'documented hooking/catching feature'); fauchard's 'back_hook_spike' (comment: 'documented catching/controlling lug, "composite glaive" typology'); dangpa's 'cross_blade_left' and dangpa's 'flank_tine_left' (both cite Muyedobotongji, a Korean martial-arts manual, for documented hook/bind/catch use).

This project data corroborates (independently of the present web research) that the guisarme's hook and analogous rear-spikes/flukes/crescents on poleaxe-class weapons are treated in the existing morphology research as historically documented catching/hooking hardware, distinct from their striking heads.

Historical function of the bill/guisarme hook (web-sourced): the bill (an English/European polearm, ancestor/relative of the guisarme naming lineage) combined 'a point to thrust with, a hook to drag with, and a spike/axe to cut with.' Multiple secondary sources describe the hook as functionally used for DISMOUNTING MOUNTED OPPONENTS -- 'good for grappling (and dismounting) mounted combatants,' with the hook 'excellent at finding a chink in the plate armour of cavalrymen... dragging the unlucky horseman off his mount to be finished off with either a sword, spear, or the bill itself.' The guisarme name itself is described as having become 'a catch-all descriptor for any weapon that included a hook on the blade... for its extra grabbage, stabbage and disarming power.'

The pole-axe (Streitaxt) is described (secondary source, referencing Talhoffer's fechtbuch) as 'a bo-length staff gripped mostly in the middle, with a head consisting of a spike, a beak and a pronged hammer or axe, with both ends used to strike and thrust or to defend, and the beak and axe used to hook.' The same source notes Talhoffer's poleaxe chapter includes 'close range grappling moves,' paralleling the longsword's Ringen am Schwert material -- i.e. the same bind-to-grapple transition documented for the sword is explicitly present for the poleaxe in Talhoffer.

Fiore's spear (lanza) material documents a specific hook-into-pull-down action, the 'Neck Hook and Pull Down from a Missed Exchange,' following the rompere di punta (breaking the thrust) play: after beating the opponent's spearhead offline, the defender strikes the opponent's neck with the flat/false edge of the spear's SHAFT (not the head), using the weapon's length as a lever to transmit force; the text then states 'the Scholar may then use the spear's shaft as a "hook" across the back of the opponent's neck to pull him to the ground,' after which 'he attacks with a thrust to whatever target is convenient.' This is explicitly framed as usable 'any time the Scholar's parry takes his spearhead offline, be that from a missed Exchange of Thrusts, a low bind with both spear heads driven to the ground, etc.' -- i.e. it is bind/parry-gated, not reachable from open measure, and works via the SHAFT as an improvised hook, not a purpose-built hook feature (Fiore's lanza in this section is a plain spear, not a guisarme/bill).

Fiore's rompere di punta text (same source) also notes, after describing the foot-pin of the opponent's weapon (see disarms finding above), that from the pinned position 'there are many different follow-on actions possible: repeated face thrusts with the heel [butt of the spear], a heel strike followed by a pass back and thrust with the spear head..., a collar throw..., or a variety of other grapples and hooks with the heel to the Player's arms and neck' -- confirming that both ends of a polearm (head and heel/butt) are documented as usable for hooking/grappling follow-ups once an opening (a beaten-aside weapon) has been created.

UNCERTAINTY FLAG / SCOPE NOTE: the primary-source detail retrievable in this pass (Mele's Getty-cited reconstruction) covers Fiore's plain spear/lanza, not a guisarme, bec-de-corbin, or halberd specifically -- but it directly documents the SAME mechanical pattern (beat/parry creates an opening -> shaft used as hook against the neck -> pull to ground -> follow-up thrust) that the project's own weapons.py comments attribute to the guisarme/ji/ranseur/partisan/fauchard/dangpa's purpose-built hook hardware. Treat the Fiore-spear citation as strong evidence for the MECHANICAL PATTERN (hook-created-opening -> pull-down -> finish) and the bill/guisarme historical descriptions as strong evidence for the DEDICATED-HOOK-HARDWARE claim; this pass did not find a single primary source describing a named play performed specifically with a guisarme's forged hook (as opposed to the improvised shaft-hook or the bill's dismounting-cavalry use).
sources: C:/Github/ttrpg-morph-rearch/designs/scene/combat_engine_v1/weapons.py (read directly; morphology-rearch Phase B2/B4 element/guard records with inline citations to Monte, Manciolino, Muyedobotongji, and general 'composite glaive typology')
http://learnfiore.org/wp-content/uploads/StrettoPlays.pdf (Gregory D. Mele -- direct citation Getty Ms. Ludwig XV.13, Carta 28V)

findings.topic: What makes a grab/pin succeed or fail -- physical/attestable factors documented
facts: TIMING/GEOMETRY OF THE INCOMING STRIKE: Fiore's Dagger 1st Master grab is described as executed 'at the apex of the strike' -- the defender's contact point with the attacker's forearm is timed to the moment of maximum extension/commitment of the incoming blow, not an arbitrary moment. The 2nd Master's strong grip is noted to work 'especially well against an overhead hammerfist style attack (mandritto/fendente)' -- i.e. success is tied to the ATTACKER'S committed, largely-linear arm trajectory giving a clean line for the grab; no equivalent claim is made for a probing or disengaging attack.

LEVERAGE/TORQUE, NOT RAW GRIP FORCE ALONE: the 1st Master disarm mechanism is described via rotational torque generated from the blocking/parrying motion (palm-down, elbow drawn to the hip) -- the lever-arm principle (a longer effective moment arm against the opponent's wrist/hand) is the stated mechanism for overpowering the opponent's grip on their own weapon, not simply stronger muscular grip.

A FREE HAND IS A STATED PRECONDITION in the German material: Ringen am Schwert's hilt-seizing and mid-blade-seizing techniques (the 'Fifth Sword Wrestle,' 'Taking a Sword') are structured around driving ONE hand over/onto the opponent's weapon or arm while the OTHER hand continues to control one's own weapon or delivers the follow-up (a mordhau/pommel strike) -- i.e. these are documented as two-part actions requiring a hand made available for the grab while retaining weapon control with the other. This is consistent with (though this pass did not find an explicit HEMA-text statement of) the general biomechanical requirement that grabbing requires a hand not otherwise occupied.

TARGET-PART EXPOSURE created by a prior action: nearly every documented grab/pin in this research is gated on a PRECEDING event that exposes the target -- a bind/crossing (German Ringen am Schwert, Fiore's Zogho Stretto), a beaten-aside/displaced weapon (Fiore's rompere di punta foot-pin and the spear neck-hook), or a committed overhead strike (Fiore's dagger masters). No source in this pass documents a grab attempted successfully against an opponent's weapon or limb that was NOT already in some way compromised (extended, displaced, or in bind contact) -- i.e. grabs are consistently opportunistic on a created opening, not a baseline always-available action.

SHAFT VS. HILT AS A GRAB TARGET (indirect evidence, not a direct HEMA-text statement of comparative ease): the German 'Taking a Sword' technique explicitly grabs the BLADE at 'the centre' (mid-blade) rather than the hilt, and does so with the off/'wrong' hand, implying a deliberate choice to seize the blade at a point away from the edge geometry and away from the opponent's hand — consistent with (but not a stated general rule for) the intuition that a long open shaft/blade offers more available grabbing surface at a safe distance from the opponent's controlling hand than a compact hilt does. The bill/guisarme's hook hardware is specifically used against a MOUNTED opponent's body/harness/armor gaps ('finding a chink in the plate armour'), not documented in the sources reviewed as hooking the opposing weapon itself. No source in this pass makes a direct comparative claim ('a haft is easier to grab than a hilt') -- this remains an inference from the differing grab-targets documented (mid-blade / hilt / wrist / neck / shaft-as-hook), not an attested fact, and should be flagged as such if used downstream.

OPPONENT'S WEAPON MORPHOLOGY AS A DOCUMENTED FACTOR ONLY IN ONE DIRECTION: the project's own weapons.py records document that CERTAIN weapons (guisarme, ji, ranseur, partisan, voulge, fauchard, dangpa) carry purpose-built hooking/catching hardware as a named, physically located feature -- meaning those weapons are morphologically better SUITED TO INITIATE a hook/catch than a weapon without such a feature (a plain spear, a sword). This is a documented asymmetry in HOOKING capability by weapon morphology; this pass found no equivalent historical claim about which weapon TYPES are easier to be grabbed/hooked (e.g. no source claims a haft is intrinsically easier for an opponent's grip to close around than a hilt in comparative terms -- only that the mid-blade/shaft is a documented alternate grab-point to the hilt).

findings.topic: Documented consequences once a weapon or limb is grabbed/pinned
facts: FOUR NAMED OUTCOME BRANCHES from a single dominant grip (Fiore Dagger 2nd Remedy Master, explicit in the text): (1) arm-break, (2) throw-to-ground, (3) disarm (take the weapon), (4) tie/restrain ('tie you in the high bind') -- presented as a menu the grappler chooses from once control is achieved, not a single fixed resolution. This is the clearest primary-adjacent evidence in this research set for grappling producing a BRANCHING outcome rather than a single effect.

WEAPON-PIN CONSEQUENCE (Fiore rompere di punta, spear/sword-derived): pinning the opponent's weapon underfoot against the ground is described as EITHER breaking the weapon outright OR holding it 'in such a way that he cannot control it any more' -- i.e. the pin is stated to remove the opponent's control of that weapon for as long as the pin holds, immediately chained (in the same play) into a follow-up strike (false-edge to the neck, then a cut to the arms/hands) while the pin is presumably still in effect or has just been released to allow the follow-up motion. The text does not describe what the pinned fighter can still do -- no counter or escape from the specific foot-pin is given in the material retrieved.

GRABBED-ARM CONSEQUENCE, TIE-UP VARIANT (German Ringeck, 'Leg-break'/'Under-break' family): the captured arm is used as the fulcrum for a throw by lifting it onto the defender's own shoulder while turning the back -- i.e. once an arm is seized, the immediate documented follow-up is a body-mechanical throw using the practitioner's own structure (shoulder, turned back) rather than a static hold; the grab is a means to an immediate throw, not typically presented as a sustained restraint (contrast with Fiore's explicit 'tie... in the high bind' branch, which IS a sustained-restraint outcome).

COUNTERS/ESCAPES ARE DOCUMENTED TO EXIST BUT WERE NOT EXTRACTED IN NAMED DETAIL for the sword-grappling material: secondary sources state Ringeck's wrestling section 'frequently includes defensive counters' and that grabbed fighters 'may counter-grip, escape through footwork manipulation, or transition to alternative wrestling positions,' and Fiore's Abrazare section is stated (secondary source, Academie Duello training wiki, no manuscript citation given) to include explicit scholar-counters showing grabbed opponents can escape arm locks by repositioning, shift to over/under grappling positions, lift the defender's arm and push toward the face, step outside to bar the knee, resist chin pressure through hand positioning, or (as a last-resort scholar) use an eye strike. These are corroborating but NOT independently verified against a primary manuscript folio in this pass -- flagged as secondary-source-only.

NO DOCUMENTED 'FREE HAND STILL ACTS NORMALLY' CLAIM: no source in this pass makes an explicit general statement about what a fighter with ONE limb/weapon grabbed can still do with their OTHER, un-grabbed hand/weapon during the grapple (e.g. 'the off-hand can still strike/parry while the primary hand is controlled'). The closest evidence is structural: Ringen am Schwert techniques are consistently two-part (one hand grabs, the other continues to act -- deliver a mordhau, retain weapon control), which implies the UN-grabbed hand remains functional, but this is an inference from technique structure, not a stated general rule, and should be flagged as such.
sources: https://www.academieduello.com/learn/resources/wiki/abrazare/ (secondary, no manuscript citation given for the counter list)

findings.topic: What the currently-inert `clinch` field should plausibly represent once wired (observation, not a proposal)
facts: This is an OBSERVATION about what the sourced material implies the field's semantics COULD honestly track, grounded only in the facts above -- it is explicitly NOT a proposed formula, gain, or scalar, per the task's scope restriction.

Every documented grab/pin in this research required a PRECEDING contact event (a bind, a beaten-aside weapon, a committed strike) -- never an unconditional 'reach out and grab from open measure' action. This suggests the historically-attested gate for a grapple ATTEMPT is state-conditional (closed measure / an existing bind / a created opening), not a standing per-weapon probability.

The morphology facts gathered (dedicated hook/catch hardware on guisarme/ji/ranseur/partisan/voulge/fauchard/dangpa; the bill/guisarme's documented dismounting-hook function; the German material's hilt-seize vs mid-blade-seize as distinct grab targets; the dagger's short haft-to-head ratio making the whole weapon graspable in one hand) all point toward `clinch` needing to be at minimum a COMPOSITE of (a) how readily the wielder's OWN weapon frees a hand or converts to a grappling aid (a 2H long-grip weapon like a guisarme/poleaxe can be choked up or partially released; a dagger occupies almost no hand at all) and (b) whether that weapon carries dedicated hooking/catching hardware (documented for 8 of the roster's weapons via mode_elements/dual_role_element) versus relying on an improvised shaft-hook (documented for Fiore's plain spear) or no hooking capability at all (a rapier, a katana).

The observed clinch VALUES in weapons.py (dagger=10, rondel=9, misericorde/stiletto=7, bec_de_corbin=8, lucerne_hammer=7 at the high end; spear=2, rapier=2, guisarme=2 at the low end) are NOT obviously consistent with the morphology facts gathered here: guisarme=2 sits at the bottom of the scale despite carrying the roster's most explicitly named, purpose-built historical hook feature ('the hook itself is the weapon's namesake catching feature'), while short daggers/rondels/misericordes sit at the top, apparently reflecting a 'short weapon = grapples well because it doesn't encumber the hands' framing (consistent with the Ringen am Schwert 2H-weapon-grapples-poorly principle already implemented via close_unwieldiness/can_choke in systems.py) rather than a 'this weapon has dedicated grabbing/hooking hardware' framing. This is a factual observation of an apparent inconsistency in the existing data against the sourced history, presented for the next design phase to resolve -- not a recommendation of which framing is correct, since both are independently attested (a free/lightly-occupied hand IS documented as a precondition for grappling in the German material; dedicated hook hardware IS documented as a distinct historical feature of certain polearms) and the historical record does not state that only one of the two should set the value.
sources: Derived from the findings above; cross-referenced against C:/Github/ttrpg-morph-rearch/designs/scene/combat_engine_v1/weapons.py and systems.py (close_unwieldiness, can_choke, grip_target) read directly in this session.

overall_confidence_note: High confidence on: the bind/crossing-gated range condition for sword-on-sword grappling in BOTH Fiore (Zogho Stretto) and German (Ringeck's 'when you bind against his sword') traditions -- independently corroborated across multiple sources in both traditions. High confidence on: Fiore's dagger 1st/2nd Remedy Masters as an open-contact (not bind-gated) wrist-grab-into-disarm/throw/tie system, with the 2nd Master's four-branch outcome being the strongest single piece of evidence for documented BRANCHING consequences of a successful grab. High confidence on: the project's own weapons.py already encoding dedicated hook/catch hardware as physically located morphology on 8 polearms, independently corroborating this research's polearm-hook findings. Medium confidence on: Ott Jud-specific technique detail (Wiktenauer blocked; relied on secondary characterization only). Medium confidence on: the general 'shaft easier to grab than hilt' intuition -- documented indirectly (mid-blade grab technique exists; dagger's compact hilt-to-blade ratio is structurally different) but no source makes the comparative claim directly, so this is flagged as an inference, not an attested fact.
